title: Why Flamingos Turn Pink From Their Diet
summary: Flamingos become pink because carotenoid pigments from algae and small aquatic animals are absorbed, chemically modified, and deposited into growing feathers and skin.

lesson.learn: Flamingos are not born bright pink. Their color develops from food. Algae, cyanobacteria, brine shrimp, and small crustaceans contain carotenoid pigments, and flamingos filter these foods from shallow salty or alkaline water. After digestion, the pigments are absorbed, carried through the body, and deposited into feathers and skin, especially when new feathers grow during molt. A richer supply of carotenoids can produce deeper pink, orange, or reddish tones, while a poor pigment supply can make birds look pale. The color is therefore a visible record of diet, metabolism, habitat, and condition.
lesson.simpler: Flamingos turn pink because they eat carotenoid-rich algae and tiny aquatic animals, then build those pigments into feathers and skin.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Flamingo color is dietary chemistry made visible, as carotenoids from algae and small crustaceans are absorbed and deposited into new feathers and skin.
lesson.deeperDive: Carotenoids are made mostly by plants, algae, and microbes, so flamingos must obtain them from food rather than manufacture them from scratch. Their bills are specialized filters, with comb-like lamellae that strain tiny food from water and mud. Different flamingo species feed on somewhat different mixtures of algae and invertebrates, but the pigment pathway is similar. Captive flamingos can fade without enough carotenoids, which is why managed diets include pigment sources. In the wild, color can also act as a condition signal because a bird with vivid plumage has found and processed enough pigment-rich food. The pink is not decoration added after the fact; it is ecology written into tissue.
lesson.funFact: A flamingo can fade toward pale pink or white if its diet lacks enough carotenoids.
references: San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance - flamingo diet and color

people: Ornithologists - scientists who study flamingo behavior and color
Animal nutritionists - specialists who design pigment-rich diets for captive flamingos
Field ecologists - researchers who study alkaline lakes and flamingo feeding
Conservation biologists - scientists who monitor flamingo habitats
places: Lake Bogoria - East African lake famous for flamingo gatherings
Alkaline lakes - pigment-rich feeding habitats for many flamingos
Bill lamellae - filter structures used to strain food
Growing feathers - tissues where dietary pigment becomes visible
years: 20th century - carotenoids become central to studies of bird coloration
1960s - pigment chemistry expands in avian nutrition research
2010s - flamingo color is widely studied as a condition signal
Present day - habitat conservation protects flamingo food webs
concepts: Astaxanthin - red-orange carotenoid found in aquatic food webs
Filter feeding - straining food from water or mud
Molt - feather replacement period when color is deposited
Condition signal - visible trait linked to health or nutrition
